title: Macroeconomic policy objectives
summary:
## The big idea Every finance minister wakes up juggling the same four balls: **growth, jobs, stable prices, and paying the country's way with the rest of the world**. This chapter defines those objectives precisely, shows how each is measured — and, crucially for exams, shows why chasing one often means dropping another. The *conflicts* are where the top marks live. ## The four core objectives (plus extras) 1. **Economic growth** — steady, *sustainable* growth of **real GDP**, raising living standards over time. 2. **Low unemployment** — as many people as possible who want work, in work. 3. **Low and stable inflation** — price stability, typically a target around **2% CPI**. 4. **A satisfactory balance of payments** — avoiding a large, persistent **current account deficit** that must be financed by borrowing or selling assets abroad. Governments increasingly add: **sustainable public finances** (a manageable budget deficit and national debt), a **fair distribution of income**, and **environmental sustainability**. ## Objective 1: economic growth Distinguish two kinds — a distinction examiners love: - **Actual growth**: real GDP rising as the economy uses existing capacity more fully — moving *toward* the PPC/LRAS. This is what recovery from recession looks like. - **Potential growth**: the capacity itself expanding — the PPC/LRAS shifting *outward* through more or better labour, capital and technology. This is the only growth that can continue once full employment is reached. Growth brings higher incomes, jobs and tax revenue — but can also bring inflation (near capacity), inequality, resource depletion and pollution. Hence the modern insistence on *sustainable* growth. ## Objective 2: low unemployment **Unemployment** = people **willing and able to work, actively seeking a job, but without one**. It is measured two ways: the **claimant count** (how many receive unemployment benefits — easy to collect, but misses those not eligible and can be manipulated) and the **ILO labour-force survey** (asks a sample directly; internationally comparable; usually higher). The **types** matter because each needs a different cure: - **Cyclical (demand-deficient)** — AD falls in a downturn and workers everywhere are laid off. Cure: boost demand. - **Structural** — industries decline and workers' **skills or location no longer match** the jobs available (coal miners in a services economy). The most stubborn kind; cure: retraining, mobility — supply-side. - **Frictional** — short spells *between* jobs; some is normal and even healthy in a changing economy. - **Seasonal** — tied to the calendar (tourism, agriculture). **Costs of unemployment:** lost output (the economy sits inside its PPC), lost incomes and skills — the longer it lasts, the more skills decay and discourage employers (**hysteresis**) — plus benefit costs, lost tax revenue, and social costs from poverty and ill health. Youth unemployment (Spain and Greece topped 50% after 2008; South Africa's remains extreme) scars a whole generation's earnings. ## Objectives 3 & 4: stable prices and external balance Both were built in Unit 4. Inflation erodes purchasing power, competitiveness and certainty; the ~2% target keeps it low *without* risking deflation. On the external side, the worry is a **large, persistent** current account deficit — financed by debt or asset sales — rather than any deficit at all. ## The conflicts — your evaluation backbone In the short run, the objectives regularly **trade off**: - **Growth/jobs vs inflation** — stimulating AD raises output and employment, but near capacity it fuels **demand-pull inflation**. - **Unemployment vs inflation** — the same tension formalised as the **short-run Phillips curve** (drawn properly at A2): lower unemployment tends to come with higher wage growth and inflation. - **Growth vs the current account** — richer consumers buy more **imports**, so booms widen trade deficits. - **Growth vs environment/equity** — output can rise while pollution worsens and the gains flow to the top. - **Fixing inflation vs everything else** — squeezing demand to cool prices slows growth and costs jobs. The mature answer is never 'the government should achieve all objectives' — it is: *given this economy's situation, which objective is most pressing, and what cost in terms of the others is acceptable?* A supply-side success — capacity growing — is prized precisely because it is the one move that can improve growth, jobs, inflation *and* trade at once (next chapter).
## ⚡ Quick revision - Four core objectives: **growth** (real GDP), **low unemployment**, **low & stable inflation (~2%)**, **satisfactory BoP** — plus sound public finances, equity, environment. - **Actual growth** = using capacity (toward PPC); **potential growth** = expanding capacity (PPC outward). - Unemployment measures: **claimant count** vs **ILO survey**. Types: **cyclical, structural, frictional, seasonal** — each needs a different cure. - Costs of unemployment: lost output & tax, benefits, skill decay (**hysteresis**), social costs. - Conflicts: growth/jobs ↔ inflation (Phillips curve); growth ↔ current account (imports); growth ↔ environment/equity. - Evaluation = **prioritise** given the context, and name the trade-off you accept.

keyTerms.definition: An increase in real GDP (actual growth) or in productive capacity (potential growth).
People willing and able to work and actively seeking a job but unable to find one.
Demand-deficient unemployment caused by a fall in AD during a downturn.
Unemployment from a mismatch of skills or location as industries change.
Short-term unemployment while workers move between jobs.
Keeping inflation low and stable, often around a 2% CPI target.
When achieving one macro objective worsens another (e.g. growth vs inflation).

diagrams: **AD/AS** to show growth (AD or LRAS shifts) and the inflation that can accompany a demand boost.
Optional at AS: sketch the **short-run Phillips curve** trade-off between unemployment and inflation (developed fully at A2).
tips: Learn the **four (to six) objectives** and be able to define and measure each (esp. the types of unemployment).
Nail the **conflicts** — growth vs inflation, unemployment vs inflation, growth vs current account — as your evaluation backbone.
Distinguish **actual vs potential growth** and **cyclical vs structural** unemployment precisely.
Note that **sustainability and equity** matter alongside raw GDP growth (links to A2 development).
Use data/context (e.g. **Spanish and Greek youth unemployment, or South Africa's high structural unemployment**) to apply the theory for higher marks.

searchTerms: growth employment inflation balance of payments
revisionNotes:
# Macro policy objectives — quick revision

**The objectives**
1. **Growth** — sustainable rises in real GDP. Actual (toward PPC) vs **potential** (PPC/LRAS outward).
2. **Low unemployment** — willing, able, seeking, jobless. Claimant count vs **ILO survey** (survey usually higher, comparable).
3. **Low & stable inflation** — ~2% CPI target.
4. **Satisfactory BoP** — no large persistent current-account deficit.
(+ sound public finances, equity, environmental sustainability.)

**Unemployment types → cures**
- **Cyclical** (AD falls) → demand-side stimulus.
- **Structural** (skills/location mismatch) → retraining, mobility, supply-side.
- **Frictional** (between jobs) → job information, some is normal.
- **Seasonal** → diversification.
- Costs: lost output/tax, benefits bill, **hysteresis**, social costs.

**The conflicts (evaluation backbone)**
- Growth & jobs ↔ **inflation** (demand-pull; short-run Phillips curve).
- Growth ↔ **current account** (booms suck in imports).
- Growth ↔ environment & equity.
- Anti-inflation squeeze ↔ growth & jobs.

**Judgement template**
- Which objective is most pressing *in this economy now*? What trade-off is acceptable? Supply-side gains dodge the conflicts — but slowly.
questions:
# Practice questions — Macroeconomic policy objectives **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Shipbuilding declines permanently in a region and former shipyard workers lack the skills demanded by new industries. Their unemployment is best described as: - A — cyclical - B — frictional - C — structural - D — seasonal
▸ Show answer

C. A permanent change in the economy's structure has left a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — the definition of structural unemployment.

**2.** An economy moves from a point inside its production possibility curve to a point on it. This represents: - A — potential economic growth - B — actual economic growth - C — a fall in living standards - D — an outward shift of the PPC
▸ Show answer

B. Using previously idle capacity raises real GDP — actual growth. Potential growth would be the PPC itself shifting outward.

**3.** Which pair of objectives is most likely to conflict in the short run? - A — low unemployment and low inflation - B — low inflation and a satisfactory balance of payments - C — economic growth and low unemployment - D — low unemployment and economic growth
▸ Show answer

A. Boosting demand to cut unemployment tends to raise wage growth and prices — the short-run Phillips curve trade-off. Growth and jobs (C/D) usually move together.

**4.** Compared with the claimant count, the ILO labour-force survey measure of unemployment is usually: - A — lower, because it excludes part-time workers - B — higher, because it includes jobless people not eligible for benefits - C — identical, by definition - D — lower, because it is based on a sample
▸ Show answer

B. The survey asks people directly whether they are seeking work, catching those who claim no benefits (e.g. new graduates, returners) — so it is normally higher and more internationally comparable.

## Paper 2 style — data response > A middle-income economy reports: real GDP growth up from 2% to 6%; unemployment down from 9% to 5%; CPI inflation up from 3% to 7%; and a current account deficit widening from 2% to 5% of GDP. The central bank wants interest-rate rises; the ministry of industry objects that 'the recovery must not be strangled'. **(a)** State the four main macroeconomic objectives. **[2]** **(b)** Explain which of the objectives this economy is currently achieving. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse **two** conflicts between objectives visible in the data. **[6]** **(d)** Assess whether the central bank is right to raise interest rates. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Growth, low unemployment, low & stable inflation, satisfactory balance of payments. [2]

(b) Achieving: strong growth (6%) and falling unemployment (5%). Missing: inflation far above a ~2% norm and rising; widening external deficit. [4]

(c) (i) Growth/jobs vs inflation: AD-led boom pulling prices up (demand-pull); (ii) growth vs current account: higher incomes sucking in imports → deficit 2%→5%. Develop both chains. [3+3]

(d) For: inflation is accelerating; acting early anchors expectations; higher rates also curb import demand → helps the external gap. Against: growth and job gains may reverse; hurts investment; blunt tool. Judgement: some tightening justified — the question is pace; note the trade-off being accepted. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain the causes of the different types of unemployment. **[8]** **(b)** Assess which type of unemployment is the most serious problem for an economy. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Cyclical (AD falls), structural (skills/location mismatch from industrial change), frictional (job search), seasonal — with an example each.

(b) Structural: long-lasting, concentrated on regions/groups, causes hysteresis — usually the strongest case. Cyclical: can be huge but responds to demand policy. Frictional/seasonal: mild, partly healthy. Judgement should weigh duration, curability and social cost.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain why governments aim for low and stable inflation rather than zero inflation. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether economic growth should be a government's most important macroeconomic objective. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Costs of high inflation (purchasing power, competitiveness, uncertainty); but zero risks deflation spirals and blocks relative wage adjustment → ~2% buffer.

(b) For growth: raises incomes, employment, tax revenue — funds all other goals. Against: inflation and current-account side-effects, inequality, environmental damage; in a crisis, stability may matter more. Judgement: growth as the long-run anchor, but *sustainable* and context-dependent.
